# Indian Standard
# METHOD FOR COMPUTATION OF FREQUENCY AND SEVERITY RATES FOR INDUSTRIAL INJURIES AND CLASSIFICATION OF INDUSTRIAL ACCIDENTS
items.value: METHOD FOR COMPUTATION OF FREQUENCY AND SEVERITY RATES FOR INDUSTRIAL INJURIES AND CLASSIFICATION OF INDUSTRIAL ACCIDENTS

items.md: ## ( Second Revision )
### 1 SCOPE

This standard prescribes basic methods for recording and classifying industrial accidents. It also includes details of work injury and gives the methods for computation of frequency, severity and incidence rates of work injuries in industrial premises. This would enable adoption of a uniform system of recording events associated with injuries and the determination of corrective action.
items.value: This standard prescribes basic methods for recording and classifying industrial accidents. It also includes details of work injury and gives the methods for computation of frequency, severity and incidence rates of work injuries in industrial premises. This would enable adoption of a uniform system of recording events associated with injuries and the determination of corrective action.

items.md: ### 2 TERMINOLOGY
For the purpose of this standard, the following definitions shall apply.
items.value: For the purpose of this standard, the following definitions shall apply.

items.md: #### 2.1 Accident
An unintended occurrence arising out of and in the course of employment of a person resulting in injury.
items.value: An unintended occurrence arising out of and in the course of employment of a person resulting in injury.

items.md: #### 2.2 Death
Fatality resulting from an accident.
#### 2.3 Disabling Injury (Lost Time Injury)
An injury causing disablement extending beyond the day of shift on which the accident occurred.
items.value: An injury causing disablement extending beyond the day of shift on which the accident occurred.

items.md: NOTE — An ideal system of accident statistics would take into account every occurrence causing injury however slight. Unfortunately, this is impracticable because the record of trivial injuries varies not only according to the number sustained but also according to the strictness with which a firm insists on this being reported. On the other hand, if an accident keeps a man away from work for a day or more, his absence is bound to be noticed. In other words, the ‘one day’ or ‘one shift’ accident has been chosen because it is a standard unit which cannot vary to a great extent from firm to firm or from year to year. This is, therefore, used for practical purposes as it is the nearest accurate figure to the ideal and is a very simple standard to use.

This is, therefore, used for practical purposes as it is the nearest accurate figure to the ideal and is a very simple standard to use.

items.md: #### 2.4 Non-disabling Injury
An injury which requires medical treatment only, without causing any disablement whether of temporary or permanent nature.
items.value: An injury which requires medical treatment only, without causing any disablement whether of temporary or permanent nature.

items.md: #### 2.5 Reportable Disabling Injury (Reportable Lost Time Injury)
items.value: 2.5 Reportable Disabling Injury (Reportable Lost Time Injury)

items.md: An injury causing death or disablement to an extent as prescribed by the relevant statute.
items.value: An injury causing death or disablement to an extent as prescribed by the relevant statute.

items.md: #### 2.6 Days of Disablement (Lost Time)

In the case of disablement of a temporary nature, the number of days on which the injured person was partially disabled is as defined in **2.7**. In the case of death or disablement of a permanent nature whether it be partial or total disablement as defined in **2.7** and **2.8** man-days lost means the charges in days of earning capacity lost due to such permanent disability or death as specified in Annex A. In other cases the day on which the injury occurred or the day the injured person returned to work are not to be included as man-days lost; but all intervening calendar days (including Sundays or, days off, or days of plant shut down) are to be included. If after resumption of work, the person injured is again disabled for any period arising out of the injury which caused his earlier disablement, the period of such subsequent disablement is also to be included in the man-days lost.

If after resumption of work, the person injured is again disabled for any period arising out of the injury which caused his earlier disablement, the period of such subsequent disablement is also to be included in the man-days lost.

items.md: #### 2.7 Partial Disablement

This is of two types: disablement of a temporary nature which reduces the earning capacity of an employed person in any employment in which he was engaged at the time of the accident resulting in the disablement; and disablement of a permanent nature, which reduces his earning capacity in every employment which he was capable of undertaking at the time.

items.md: #### 2.8 Total Disablement

Disablement, whether of a temporary or permanent nature, which incapacitates a workman for all work which he was capable of performing at the time of the accident resulting in such disablement, provided that permanent total disablement shall be deemed to result from every type of injury specified in Part A of Annex A or from any combination of injuries specified in Part B of Annex A where the aggregate percentage of the loss of earning capacity, as specified in that part against those injuries, amounts to one hundred percent.

items.md: ## 3 CALCULATION OF MAN-HOURS WORKED

Man-hours worked shall be calculated from the pay roll or time clock recorded including overtime. When this is not feasible, the same shall be estimated by multiplying the total man-days worked for the period covered by the number of hours worked per day. The total number of man-days for a period is the sum of the number of men at work on each day of the period. If the daily hours vary from department to department separate estimates shall be made for each department and the result added together. When actual man-hours are not used, the basis on which the estimates are made shall be indicated.

If the daily hours vary from department to department separate estimates shall be made for each department and the result added together. When actual man-hours are not used, the basis on which the estimates are made shall be indicated.

items.md: ## 5 ASSESSMENT OF WORK INJURY
### 5.1 Basis of Assessment
#### 5.1.1 General

Thorough investigation of all factors relating to the occurrence of each reported injury is essential. Determination as to whether or not the injury should be considered a work injury under the provisions of this standard shall be based on the evidence collected during investigations.
items.value: Thorough investigation of all factors relating to the occurrence of each reported injury is essential. Determination as to whether or not the injury should be considered a work injury under the provisions of this standard shall be based on the evidence collected during investigations.

items.md: #### 5.1.2 Evidence
The evidence to be considered in determining whether or not the reported injury should be considered a work injury may include, but not necessarily be limited to, the following:
items.value: The evidence to be considered in determining whether or not the reported injury should be considered a work injury may include, but not necessarily be limited to, the following:

items.md: a) Facts resulting from investigation of the injured employee's work activities and working environment to which the injury might be related;
items.value: a) Facts resulting from investigation of the injured employee's work activities and working environment to which the injury might be related;

items.md: b) Statements (written if possible) of the injured employee, fellow employees, witnesses and supervisors;
items.value: b) Statements (written if possible) of the injured employee, fellow employees, witnesses and supervisors;

items.md: c) Medical reports acceptable to the authority classifying the work injury; and
items.value: c) Medical reports acceptable to the authority classifying the work injury; and

items.md: d) Facts concerning the injured employee's work activity for other employers, and other off-the-job activities, injuries and illnesses.
items.value: d) Facts concerning the injured employee's work activity for other employers, and other off-the-job activities, injuries and illnesses.

items.md: ### 5.2 Assessment of Special Cases
#### 5.2.1 General

Before inclusion in the record special case should be assessed. Clauses 5.2.2 to 5.2.15 are intended to assist in such assessment but these provisions/rules should not be used to exclude a genuine work injury from the record.

items.md: # 6 COMPUTATION OF FREQUENCY, SEVERITY AND INCIDENCE RATES
items.value: 6 COMPUTATION OF FREQUENCY, SEVERITY AND INCIDENCE RATES

items.md: ## 6.1 Frequency Rate
The frequency rate shall be calculated both for lost time injury and reportable lost time injury as follows:
items.value: The frequency rate shall be calculated both for lost time injury and reportable lost time injury as follows:

items.md: $$F\_A = \frac{\text{Number of lost time injury} \times 1,000,000}{\text{Man-hours worked}}$$
$$F\_B = \frac{\text{Number of reportable lost time injury} \times 1,000,000}{\text{Man-hours worked}}$$
items.value: $$F_B = \frac{\text{Number of reportable lost time injury} \times 1,000,000}{\text{Man-hours worked}}$$

items.md:
NOTES
**1** If the injury does not cause loss of time in the period in which it occurs but in a subsequent period, the injury should be included in the frequency rate of the period in which the loss of time begins.
**2** If an injury causes intermittent loss of time, it should only be included in the frequency rate once, that is, when the first loss of time occurs.
**3** Since frequency rate $F\_B$ is based on the lost time injuries reportable to the statutory authorities, it may be used for official purposes only. In all other cases, frequency rate $F\_A$ should be used for comparison purposes.
items.value:

3 Since frequency rate $F_B$ is based on the lost time injuries reportable to the statutory authorities, it may be used for official purposes only. In all other cases, frequency rate $F_A$ should be used for comparison purposes.

items.md: ## 6.2 Severity Rate
The severity rate shall be calculated from man-days lost both of lost time injury and reportable lost time injury as follows:
items.value: The severity rate shall be calculated from man-days lost both of lost time injury and reportable lost time injury as follows:

items.md: $$S\_A = \frac{\text{Man-days lost due to lost time injury} \times 1,000,000}{\text{Man-hours worked}}$$
items.value: $$S_A = \frac{\text{Man-days lost due to lost time injury} \times 1,000,000}{\text{Man-hours worked}}$$

items.md: $$S\_B = \frac{\text{Man-days lost due to reportable lost time injury} \times 1,000,000}{\text{Man-hours worked}}$$
items.value: $$S_B = \frac{\text{Man-days lost due to reportable lost time injury} \times 1,000,000}{\text{Man-hours worked}}$$

items.md: NOTE — Since severity rate $S\_B$ is based on the lost time injuries reportable to the statutory authorities, it should be used for official purposes only. In all other cases severity rate $S\_A$ should be used for comparison purposes.

items.md: ### 6.3 Incidence Rate
**6.3.1** General incidence rate is the ratio of the number of injuries to the number of persons during the period under review. It is expressed as the number of injuries per 1 000 persons employed.
items.value: 6.3.1 General incidence rate is the ratio of the number of injuries to the number of persons during the period under review. It is expressed as the number of injuries per 1 000 persons employed.

items.md: The incidence rate may be calculated both for lost-time injuries and reportable lost-time injuries as follows:
items.value: The incidence rate may be calculated both for lost-time injuries and reportable lost-time injuries as follows:

items.md:
Lost-time injury incidence rate
$$= \frac{\text{Number of lost time injuries} \times 1\ 000}{\text{Average number of persons employed}}$$
items.value:
Lost-time injury incidence rate
$$= \frac{\text{Number of lost time injuries} \times 1\ 000}{\text{Average number of persons employed}}$$

items.md:
Injury incidence rate
$$= \frac{\text{Number of reportable lost-time injuries} \times 1\ 000}{\text{Average number of persons employed}}$$
items.value:
Injury incidence rate
$$= \frac{\text{Number of reportable lost-time injuries} \times 1\ 000}{\text{Average number of persons employed}}$$

items.md: ### 6.4 Statistical Period

Rates for any period, that is month, quarter or year shall include injuries which occurred during the period, together with any injuries which occurred in the previous 12 months and which have not already been included in earlier calculations.
items.value: Rates for any period, that is month, quarter or year shall include injuries which occurred during the period, together with any injuries which occurred in the previous 12 months and which have not already been included in earlier calculations.

items.md: **6.4.1** An injury which occurred in a previous period and which did not cause lost time at the time of occurrence, but caused lost time in the current period, shall be included as a lost-time injury in the current period.
items.value: 6.4.1 An injury which occurred in a previous period and which did not cause lost time at the time of occurrence, but caused lost time in the current period, shall be included as a lost-time injury in the current period.

items.md: **6.4.2** When calculating duration rates, the time lost for the period shall include time lost in the current period caused by injuries which occurred in previous period.
items.value: 6.4.2 When calculating duration rates, the time lost for the period shall include time lost in the current period caused by injuries which occurred in previous period.

Includes simple fractures; fractures with injuries to soft parts of the body (compound fracture); fractures with injuries to articulations (dislocations, etc); fractures with internal or nerve injuries.

*320 Dislocations*
Includes subluxations and displacements.
Excludes fracture dislocations (310).
*325 Sprains and Strains*
Includes, unless associated with an open wound, the ruptures, tears, and locations of muscles, tendons, ligaments and joints, as well as hernias due to over exertion.
items.value: Includes, unless associated with an open wound, the ruptures, tears, and locations of muscles, tendons, ligaments and joints, as well as hernias due to over exertion.

items.md: *330 Concussions and Other Internal Injuries*
Includes, unless fractures are involved, all internal concussions, haemorrhages, lacerations, ruptures.
items.value: Includes, unless fractures are involved, all internal concussions, haemorrhages, lacerations, ruptures.

items.md: Excludes those injuries with fractures (310).
*340 Amputations and Enucleations*
Includes traumatic avulsion of eye.
*341 Other Wounds*
Includes lacerations, open wounds, cuts, contusions with wounds, scalp wounds, as well as loss of nails or ears; includes wounds involving injury to nerves.
items.value: Includes lacerations, open wounds, cuts, contusions with wounds, scalp wounds, as well as loss of nails or ears; includes wounds involving injury to nerves.

items.md: Excludes traumatic amputations, enucleations; avulsion of eye (340) compound fracture (310); burns with open wounds (360); superficial injuries (350).
items.value: Excludes traumatic amputations, enucleations; avulsion of eye (340) compound fracture (310); burns with open wounds (360); superficial injuries (350).

items.md: *350 Superficial Injuries*
Includes abrasions, scratches, blisters, bites of non-venomous insects, superficial wounds; also includes superficial injuries due to foreign bodies entering in the eye.
items.value: Includes abrasions, scratches, blisters, bites of non-venomous insects, superficial wounds; also includes superficial injuries due to foreign bodies entering in the eye.

items.md: *355 Contusions and crushings*
Includes haemarthrosis, haematoma and bruises; contusions and crushing with fracture (310); and contusions and crushings with an open wound (341).
items.value: Includes haemarthrosis, haematoma and bruises; contusions and crushing with fracture (310); and contusions and crushings with an open wound (341).

items.md: *360 Burns*
Includes burns from hot objects; from fire; scales; friction burns; radiation burns (infra-red); chemical burns (external burns only); burns with open wounds.
items.value: Includes burns from hot objects; from fire; scales; friction burns; radiation burns (infra-red); chemical burns (external burns only); burns with open wounds.

items.md: Excludes burns due to swallowing a corrosive or caustic substance (370); sunburn (380); effects of lighting (380); burns due to electric current (382); and radiation effects other than burns (383).
items.value: Excludes burns due to swallowing a corrosive or caustic substance (370); sunburn (380); effects of lighting (380); burns due to electric current (382); and radiation effects other than burns (383).

items.md: *370 Acute Poisonings*

Includes the effects of the injection, ingestion, absorption or inhalation of toxic, corrosive or caustic substances; bites of venomous insects or animals; asphyxiation by carbon monoxide or other toxic gases.
items.value: Includes the effects of the injection, ingestion, absorption or inhalation of toxic, corrosive or caustic substances; bites of venomous insects or animals; asphyxiation by carbon monoxide or other toxic gases.

items.md: Exclude external chemical burns (360).
*380 Effects of weather, Exposure and Related Conditions*
items.value: 380 Effects of weather, Exposure and Related Conditions

items.md: Includes effects of reduced temperature (frost-bite); the effect of heat and isolation (heat strokes, sunstrokes); barotrauma (effects of high altitude, decompression caused due to working in compressed air well); the effects of lightning; sound trauma (total or partial loss of hearing as a separate injury, not a sequel to another injury).
items.value: Includes effects of reduced temperature (frost-bite); the effect of heat and isolation (heat strokes, sunstrokes); barotrauma (effects of high altitude, decompression caused due to working in compressed air well); the effects of lightning; sound trauma (total or partial loss of hearing as a separate injury, not a sequel to another injury).

items.md: *381 Asphyxia*

Includes drawing asphyxiation or suffocation by compression, constriction or strangulations; also includes asphyxiation by suppression or reduction of oxygen in the surrounding atmosphere and asphyxiation by foreign bodies in the respiratory tract.
items.value: Includes drawing asphyxiation or suffocation by compression, constriction or strangulations; also includes asphyxiation by suppression or reduction of oxygen in the surrounding atmosphere and asphyxiation by foreign bodies in the respiratory tract.

items.md: Excludes asphyxiation by carbon monoxide or other toxic gases (370).
items.value: Excludes asphyxiation by carbon monoxide or other toxic gases (370).

items.md: *382 Effects of Electric Currents*
Includes electrocution; electrical shock and burn due to electric current.
items.value: Includes electrocution; electrical shock and burn due to electric current.

items.md: Excludes burns caused by electrical heating appliances (370) and the effects of lightning (380).
items.value: Excludes burns caused by electrical heating appliances (370) and the effects of lightning (380).

items.md: *383 Effects of Radiations*
Includes effects caused by X-rays, radio-active substances, ultra-violet rays, ionising radiations.
items.value: Includes effects caused by X-rays, radio-active substances, ultra-violet rays, ionising radiations.

items.md: Excludes burns due to radiations (360) and sunstrokes (380).
items.value: Excludes burns due to radiations (360) and sunstrokes (380).

items.md: *390 Multiple Injuries of Different Nature*
This group should be used only for case where the injured person sustained several injuries of different nature and no injury is obviously more severe than the others.
items.value: This group should be used only for case where the injured person sustained several injuries of different nature and no injury is obviously more severe than the others.

items.md: In case of multiple injuries suffered in one accident where one of the injuries is obviously more severe than the others, then this accident should be classified in the group corresponding to the nature of the more obviously severe injury.
items.value: In case of multiple injuries suffered in one accident where one of the injuries is obviously more severe than the others, then this accident should be classified in the group corresponding to the nature of the more obviously severe injury.

items.md: *399 Others and Unspecified Injuries*

This group should only be used to classify injuries which cannot be classified elsewhere. Includes various early complications of trauma and pathological reaction which should be classified in this group only when the nature of the antecedent injury is unknown.

**NOTE —** The groups relating to multiple locations (see Code No. 46) should be used only to classify cases where the victim suffers from several injuries to different parts of the body and no injury obviously is more severe than the others. When in an accident which caused multiple injuries located at different parts of the body one of these injuries is obviously more severe than the others, this accident, should be classified in the group corresponding to the location of the obviously more severe injury. For example, a fracture of the leg accompanied by the scratch of the hand should be classified in group 454.

items.md: **Review of Indian Standards**

Amendments are issued to standards as the need arises on the basis of comments. Standards are also reviewed periodically; a standard along with amendments is reaffirmed when such review indicates that no changes are needed; if the review indicates that changes are needed, it is taken up for revision.
